Hey, I'm Edward Omondi Software Engineer

Full-stack Software Engineer with 5+ years of experience designing, building, and deploying scalable web and mobile applications. I specialise in Java/Spring ecosystems and modern frameworks like Angular and Nuxt (Vue), combining a Mechatronics Engineering background to bridge hardware-software gaps — from REST APIs to IoT controllers.
Thoughts on software engineering, architecture, and building for the real world
I build scalable full-stack applications where clean architecture meets user-centric design. Based in Nairobi, delivering production-grade solutions from IoT controllers to SaaS platforms.
Common questions about my work, process, and experience.
What technologies do you specialise in?

My core stack is Java (Spring Boot / Jakarta EE) on the backend, paired with Angular or Nuxt (Vue) on the frontend, with PostgreSQL as my go-to database. I also work with Flutter for mobile, Python for scripting and IoT (Raspberry Pi), Firebase, and DevOps tooling for CI/CD on Linux servers.
Do you do both frontend and backend development?

Yes, I'm a full-stack engineer. I design and implement REST APIs and microservices in Java/Spring, build responsive frontends in Angular or Nuxt/Vue, and handle everything in between — database design, authentication (Keycloak/OAuth 2.0), deployment, and server configuration.
Have you worked with IoT or hardware projects?

Yes — my Mechatronics Engineering background gives me a unique edge here. I've built IoT systems using Raspberry Pi and Python, including an RFID-based point tracking system and a smart solenoid lock controller connected to a web application.
Are you available for freelance or contract work?

Yes, I'm open to freelance projects, contract engagements, and consulting. I've previously delivered end-to-end applications for clients across Kenya and Mexico. Feel free to reach out via email at ewomondi@gmail.com.
What does your development process look like?

I follow an iterative approach: requirements gathering, architecture design, development with CI/CD from day one, testing, and deployment on bare metal or virtual Linux servers. I keep clients in the loop throughout and prioritise maintainability and scalability.
Can you handle full project delivery from design to deployment?

Absolutely. I've delivered complete projects end-to-end including UI/UX design, frontend and backend development, database setup, authentication, server configuration, and CI/CD automation. I can take a project from idea to production.

I hold a BSc in Mechatronics Engineering from JKUAT (Nairobi), graduating with Upper Second Class Honours. This multidisciplinary background — spanning mechanical, electrical, and software systems — informs how I approach complex engineering problems holistically.
What do you enjoy most about software engineering?

I love the challenge of designing systems that solve real problems elegantly. Whether it's a gift card loyalty platform, a smart lock controller, or a bulk SMS system, I find the most satisfaction in shipping something that genuinely works for real users in the real world.
What are your hobbies outside of work?

Outside of engineering, I'm passionate about cars and car culture, board games, and music. I also follow the latest in tech and enjoy the occasional deep dive into cybersecurity — I completed my Certified Ethical Hacking (CEH) certification in late 2024.
Welcome to my portfolio! I'm Edward Omondi, a full-stack Software Engineer based in Nairobi, Kenya, specialising in Java/Spring and modern web frameworks like Angular and Nuxt.

Edward has an exceptional ability to deliver complex full-stack features on time without sacrificing quality. His deep knowledge of Spring Boot and Angular made our product launch seamless, and his CI/CD automation saved us weeks of manual deployment work.

Working with Edward on Cardly was a fantastic experience. He designed and built our entire Vue/Spring Boot stack from scratch, delivering a production-ready gift card platform that scales effortlessly. His technical breadth — from database design to OAuth integration — is remarkable.

After years of building Spring Boot APIs for production systems — from gift card platforms to IoT-connected web applications — I've developed patterns that consistently produce clean, maintainable, and scalable REST services. Here's what I've learned.
The most important thing you can do is enforce strict layering:
. Controllers handle HTTP concerns only. Services contain business logic. Repositories abstract data access. Mixing these creates code that's impossible to test or maintain at scale.
and Bean Validation annotations on your request DTOs — not your entities. Your API surface is where bad data enters; that's where validation belongs. Never let invalid data reach your service layer.
language-java shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight
methods gives you one place to map exceptions to consistent HTTP responses. This keeps controllers clean and ensures uniform error payloads across your entire API.

Integrating Keycloak for OAuth 2.0 authentication is something I set up at project inception, not as an afterthought. Spring Security's resource server configuration makes it straightforward to protect endpoints and extract JWT claims for authorisation:
public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {

Never expose JPA entities directly from your API. DTOs give you control over your API contract independent of your database schema, prevent accidental field exposure, and eliminate lazy-loading exceptions in responses.
These patterns have served me well across multiple production deployments. The key is consistency — apply them uniformly from the start, and your API will stay maintainable as it grows.

State management is where many Angular projects become unnecessarily complex. After building several production Angular applications — including the Aims property management system and Herdwise — here's the approach I've settled on.
For most application state, a well-designed service with
observables is all you need. Components subscribe to the stream, the service manages transitions — no store libraries required.
language-typescript shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight
Angular's built-in signals are now my first choice for component-level and simple shared state. They're synchronous, fine-grained, and require zero boilerplate:

Only introduce a state management library if you genuinely need time-travel debugging, complex cross-module state sharing, or a very large team that benefits from strict conventions. For most projects I've worked on — including Aims and Herdwise — that bar is never reached.
The real win is keeping your components thin. Push logic into services, keep templates declarative, and your application stays testable and understandable even as it grows.
on all components. Combined with signals or observables, this gives you near-optimal rendering performance without any manual optimisation.
Keep it simple. Reach for complexity only when simple provably breaks.

Authentication is one of those things you really don't want to get wrong. After implementing Keycloak across multiple production systems at Technovation Ventures, I've developed a reliable pattern for integrating it with Spring Boot backends and Angular frontends.

Keycloak gives you a full OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect identity provider you can self-host. You get user management, social logins, MFA, and fine-grained authorisation — without building any of it yourself. For B2B SaaS or multi-tenant systems, it's hard to beat.
Add the Spring Security OAuth2 resource server dependency and configure your
language-yaml shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter material-theme material-theme-palenight

spring:
  security:
    oauth2:
      resourceserver:
        jwt:
          issuer-uri: https://your-keycloak-domain/realms/your-realm
Then configure your security filter chain to protect routes:
On the frontend, an HTTP interceptor attaches the JWT to every outgoing request automatically:
Keycloak embeds roles in JWT claims. On the Spring Boot side, extract these in a custom JWT converter to use them with

For multi-tenant applications like Cardly, each organisation gets its own Keycloak realm. The backend reads the realm from the JWT issuer claim, allowing a single API to serve multiple tenants with complete data isolation.
This setup has been running in production across several projects — it's robust, scales well, and keeps auth cleanly separated from business logic.
